intro:
The remnant is the faithful few whom God preserves through judgment to carry forward his purpose. The word evokes what is left over after a great loss — a surviving fragment of a people that might have been wholly destroyed. Throughout Scripture this small surviving company is the thread by which God keeps his promises alive when the mass of his people falls under wrath, so that judgment is never God's last word: he always leaves a seed.

The pattern appears early. Through the flood God preserves Noah and his household, "only Noah was left, and those who were with him" (Genesis 7:23); and Joseph tells his brothers that God sent him ahead "to preserve for you a remnant on earth" (Genesis 45:7). When Elijah despairs of being the last believer, the LORD answers that he has kept seven thousand who have not bowed to Baal (1 Kings 19:18) — the remnant is God's, not the prophet's, and larger than he can see.

The prophets make the remnant a settled hope. Isaiah names his son Shear-jashub, "a remnant shall return," and promises that though Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will return to "the Mighty God" (Isaiah 10:21–22); apart from this surviving seed Judah would have become like Sodom (Isaiah 1:9). Micah pictures the LORD gathering "the remnant of Israel" like a flock (Micah 2:12), and Zephaniah foresees a humble remnant that does no wrong and takes refuge in the name of the LORD (Zephaniah 3:13). The exiles who return from Babylon are themselves this surviving remnant, kept for restoration.

The shadow finds its substance in the gospel age. Paul, citing Isaiah, declares that "though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, only a remnant of them will be saved" (Romans 9:27), and that "at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace" (Romans 11:5). This believing remnant of Israel — Jews who receive their Messiah — forms the root into which believing Gentiles are grafted, so that the church is the remnant-people, preserved and gathered not by their own merit but by sovereign grace. What the prophets saw as a surviving fragment of Israel becomes, in Christ, the nucleus of a redeemed humanity drawn from every nation.

So the remnant teaches that God's cause never fails for lack of numbers. He preserves a faithful people through every judgment, and the smallness of the remnant only magnifies the grace that keeps it.
